revised_position: Elena is directionally right — for unidirectional notifications, SSE matches the data flow better than WebSocket. But her claim that 'SSE rides on HTTP, which means every intermediary already knows how to handle it' is the exact kind of 'just works' certainty my background trains me to distrust. SSE over HTTP/1.1 works in the spec sense while failing in the real-time sense, because every intermediary between your server and client potentially buffers that chunked response. I've traced SSE connections through an nginx ingress with proxy_buffering on, a Cloudflare edge, a corporate Zscaler proxy, and a Sophos UTM — and the end-to-end latency looked nothing like 'real-time' because three layers were buffering the stream. SSE over HTTP/2 also has a critical failure mode Elena doesn't address: backpressure. If the client can't keep up, TCP receive buffer fills, the HTTP/2 flow control window closes, and that blocks ALL multiplexed streams sharing that connection — not just the slow SSE stream. I've seen this take down colocated API traffic on the same HTTP/2 session. Elena is also too dismissive of the 'but you might need bidirectional later' argument. I've watched teams retrofit acknowledgments onto SSE with polling loops that were architecturally worse than just using WebSocket from day one. That said, she's right about WebSocket's operational tax — the ping/pong keepalive configuration at every proxy hop, the upgrade handling, the silent connection drops at exactly the ALB idle timeout. I've been the one tracing TCP flows through an F5 to find where the WebSocket closed. My position: SSE is the right default, but you must test it against your actual proxy chain end-to-end before committing, and you must have a plan for backpressure. If you cannot control the proxy topology or need any bidirectional signaling, WebSocket — despite its higher complexity ceiling — may actually be the simpler operational choice because its failure modes are at least documented in the RFC.

conceded_to.point: SSE's built-in reconnection with Last-Event-ID and standard EventSource behavior is genuinely better than every team implementing their own WebSocket reconnection logic — most implementations do get exponential backoff wrong, and I've seen the reconnect storms.

WebSocket's hidden operational tax at scale is real. Every hop needs explicit configuration, and misconfiguration at any single hop causes connection drops that are disproportionately hard to diagnose compared to SSE's HTTP status code failures.
For purely unidirectional notification payloads (order fills, price updates), SSE's model maps to the data flow with less framing overhead than WebSocket's bidirectional channel.

disagrees_with.point: SSE 'rides on HTTP so every intermediary knows how to handle it' — this is the claim that needs the most caveat from someone who audits proxy chains for a living. Intermediaries handle HTTP, yes, but they also buffer chunked responses, cache GET responses, terminate HTTP/2 and forward over HTTP/1.1 (killing multiplexing), and apply timeouts designed for request-response, not streaming. SSE works through proxies, but it doesn't work in real-time through proxies unless you've tested every layer.

SSE's HTTP/2 multiplexing advantage is true at the protocol level but frequently lost in real deployments because the CDN terminates HTTP/2 at the edge and forwards to origin over HTTP/1.1. Your multiple EventSource streams look like independent connections to the origin server, and the multiplexing benefit evaporates.

The backpressure failure mode of SSE over HTTP/2 is not addressed. A slow consumer blocks their HTTP/2 stream, which blocks the flow control window for that connection, which can impact other, unrelated API traffic sharing the same HTTP/2 session. This is a class of failure unique to SSE that WebSocket doesn't have because each WebSocket is its own TCP connection.

new_insights: The decisive question isn't protocol capabilities — it's proxy topology control. If you control the full path from server to client (internal service mesh, known CDN config, no corporate MITM proxies), SSE's simplicity wins. If your traffic traverses uncontrolled intermediaries, you need to test, not assume.

SSE's 'no return channel' is an advantage only if you are absolutely certain you'll never need acknowledgments, channel subscriptions, or backpressure signals. The cost of retrofitting bidirectional semantics onto SSE (polling + secondary channel) can exceed the cost of WebSocket's complexity from day one.

HTTP/2 connection coalescing creates a risk profile for SSE that doesn't exist with WebSocket: one misbehaving SSE stream on a coalesced connection can degrade the performance of all other streams sharing that connection. This is particularly dangerous in browser environments where multiple origins may share an HTTP/2 connection.

The real operational difference is failure mode signatures. SSE failures produce HTTP-level diagnostics (status codes, proxy error pages) that existing monitoring tooling understands. WebSocket failures produce close frame codes that require specialized tooling to capture and interpret. This makes SSE significantly easier to operate at the cost of being significantly harder to predict the failure conditions of.
